topic: Post-patch re-review of WIN-062 Tesla 1.574c. Decider self-apply (commit 20f6d44) resolved c4 H1 (source attribution Tesla 1905 patent -> 1899 Colorado Springs Notes), H2 ('verbatim' removed), H3 ('patent value' -> 'published velocity'). Fresh c5 cycle on the rewritten detail_verdict_text.
change_summary: Decider commit 20f6d44 rewrote the second paragraph of detail_verdict_text to fix the 1905-vs-1899 Tesla source attribution (c4 H1), drop the 'adopts this published number verbatim' phrase (c4 H2), and replace 'century-old patent value' with 'century-old published velocity' (c4 H3). detail_verdict_text length 931 -> 1201 chars (+270, because the corrected chronology is longer). detail_evidence unchanged at 5130. verdict unchanged ('Misleading'). claim/finding/TLDRs unchanged. The three c4 major/moderate holes are fully resolved in the current text.
our_argument_summary: WIN-062 (verdict: Misleading) now argues, in paragraph 1 (scientific critique): (a) Tesla's US Patent 787412 depicts a spherical Earth in Figure 1 and the patent itself describes surface wave propagation on a globe, (b) ~1.57c is a published ELF/VLF waveguide phase velocity on the spherical Earth-ionosphere system (Galejs 1966, DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171), (c) the dome's disc_diameter (40,030 km) was fitted to globe circumference (40,075 km) via test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py, so numerical agreement is construction-by-fitting, not confirmation. Paragraph 2 (post-hoc test, rewritten): the underlying Tesla measurement (~1.5c; 0.08484 s round-trip) was recorded in the 1899 Colorado Springs experiments and published posthumously in 1978 (Nikola Tesla Museum, Beograd); the 1905 US Patent 787412 describes the same method and depicts a sphere; the value has been in the scientific record for over a century; the dome adopts it, re-expresses as 1.574c using the dome disc diameter, and labels CONFIRMED without pre-registration; the Part 3.1b carve-out fails on condition (2) 'not plausibly known to the author at write-time.' The EXP-181 paragraph in detail_evidence credits the 2026-04-17 registry_integrity_notes machine-layer concession while flagging the persisting visible wins.html duplication, framed as one of at least three parallel acknowledge-without-fix moves in a 24-hour window.

relevant_files: data/wins.json entry id='062' (verdict 'Misleading'; detail_verdict_text length 1201, detail_evidence length 5130)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 23 ('va = 1.574c (Tesla longitudinal wave speed)'), line 58 ('Derivation: f = va/(2xr_disc), va=1.574c'), line 207 ('Source: Tesla Colorado Springs Notes 1899')
raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54 ('WIN-001 ... Source: US Patent 787412 (1905)') — confirms patent is the dome's source for WIN-001 (11.78 Hz), not WIN-062
git commit 20f6d44 (Decider 2026-04-20T18 self-apply) — the patch under review
monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-062.c4.json (c4 review identifying H1/H2/H3 that this patch resolves)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No dome monitor.py domain for Tesla wave speed. The dome attributes the 1.574c value to Tesla's 1899 Colorado Springs Notes (per raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207). 1.574c is produced by multiplying Tesla's 0.08484 s round-trip by the dome's fitted disc_diameter (40,030 km). No external API fetch, no live comparison.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — this patch is prose-only; no code pipeline changed. Monitoring tag remains 'none'.
data_sources_used: Tesla 1899 Colorado Springs Notes (published posthumously by the Nikola Tesla Museum, Beograd 1978) — the dome's own source per live-power Domain 2
Tesla US Patent 787412 (filed 1900, issued 1905) — spherical-Earth drawings used in our scientific critique; dome's source for WIN-001 (different WIN)
Dome repository scripts test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py (per c2 citation check) — fit disc_diameter to globe circumference
Galejs 1966 (DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) — Earth-ionosphere waveguide phase-velocity kernel of truth
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The rewritten paragraph tightens the post-hoc framing but leaves three rhetorical openings for a defender: (1) the carve-out has three conjunctive conditions and the verdict cites only condition (2); (2) 'in the scientific record for over a century' technically describes the 1899 manuscript measurement but the formal publication is 1978 (48 years) — a pedantic defender can push the 'scientific record' phrase; (3) 'without pre-registering the expected value' invites the OTS rebuttal without addressing the canary (OTS is on the wrong document). None of these defeat the Misleading verdict; see holes_found.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No new code claims introduced. The pre-existing code_analysis tags (monitoring=none, relabels_standard=true, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false) are still correct; c4 established them and this patch does not touch them.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py domain for Tesla wave speed in the dome repo. The dome's raw-text/08-live-power.txt live dashboard shows Tesla Longitudinal Frequency (Domain 2) as a separate readout with hardcoded 'Source: Tesla Colorado Springs Notes 1899'; no external API fetch, no live comparison for the 1.574c velocity itself. Tag unchanged across c3, c4, c5.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: ~1.57c is the published phase velocity for ELF/VLF waves in the spherical Earth-ionosphere waveguide (Galejs 1966, DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171). The dome keeps the value and relabels the mechanism 'spherical waveguide' -> 'flat-disc aether.' No new numerical prediction. Tag unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Tesla's 0.08484 s round-trip and ~1.5c velocity were recorded in his 1899 Colorado Springs experiments. Those notes were circulated in Tesla biographies for decades and formally published by the Nikola Tesla Museum (Beograd) in 1978. Either way, public availability predates the dome's adoption by over a century (if dating to the 1899 measurement) or by ~48 years (if dating to the 1978 publication). The dome's 1.574c 'prediction' post-dates both. No independent pre-registration of the dome's value in any timestamping venue.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The disc_diameter (40,030 km) was fitted to globe circumference (40,075 km, WGS84-derived) by repository scripts test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py (c2 citation check). 1.574c is produced by combining that fitted disc_diameter with Tesla's 0.08484 s round-trip — globe-circumference -> fitted disc_diameter -> Tesla time -> dome 'prediction.' No dome-geometry-first derivation.

holes_found.description: Carve-out undersell (unchanged from c4 H4). The rewritten paragraph 2 still invokes only condition (2) of the Part 3.1b carve-out ('not plausibly known to the author at write-time'). Part 3.1b lists three conjunctive conditions (unique-to-dome derivation, not-plausibly-known data, derivation-plus-value published together). A defender who reads Part 3.1b in full can reply: 'Your own text says the carve-out needs all three conditions; you only addressed one, so strictly speaking you've only shown one leg of the test fails.' The practical answer is that condition (1) also fails for WIN-062 — the disc_diameter was fitted to globe circumference (see detail_evidence) — so the carve-out fails on at least two legs. Citing only (2) understates the case and preserves a small defender opening. Severity minor because the argument still lands on condition (2) alone; it's a strengthening opportunity, not an error.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: append a half-sentence to the final sentence, e.g. '... a century-old published velocity does not qualify — and the derivation is not unique to the dome either, since the disc_diameter was itself fitted to globe circumference.' One sentence closes both legs.

holes_found.description: Sourcing-window phrasing. 'The value has been in the scientific record for over a century' is accurate for the 1899 measurement but glosses the 1899 manuscript / 1978 posthumous publication distinction. A defender pressing literalism can say: 'Your own paragraph says posthumous publication in 1978 — that is 48 years, not a century. The 1899 notes were private until 1978.' The substantive reply is that the measurement itself is 127 years old and the 1978 posthumous publication is a transcription of the 1899 notebooks, not a new scientific result. The current phrasing invites the quibble. Severity minor — the chronology paragraph tells the reader the full story; only the one-liner conflation is loose.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional tighten: 'The measurement is over a century old and has been publicly accessible since the 1978 posthumous publication at the latest' — or simply 'the value is over a century old and was in print decades before the dome adopted it.' Not blocking; a defender challenging this phrase is scoring a point on words, not substance.

holes_found.description: Pre-registration phrase invites OTS rebuttal without addressing the canary. 'labels it CONFIRMED, without pre-registering the expected value' is literally correct (the dome's OpenTimestamps timestamps its status_history.json — the observation ledger, not the predictions), but a defender can respond: 'We DO pre-register via OpenTimestamps on our status_history.json; see the timestamp-scope block.' Without a cross-reference to the Timestamp Error tab, the sentence leaves that retort un-neutralized inside this WIN. The c4 review did not flag this, so it is a new observation specific to the rewritten paragraph.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: after 'without pre-registering the expected value', add 'in any independent timestamping venue (see Timestamp Error tab for why the dome's own OTS applies to status_history.json rather than to the predictions themselves)'. This chains the WIN to the existing canary argument and removes the opening. Low priority — the cross-ref exists elsewhere in the page — but one short phrase closes it here.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c4 H5 unchanged in detail_evidence. 'one of at least three parallel acknowledge-without-fix moves shipped inside a 24-hour window on 2026-04-17' hedges with 'at least three' while the paragraph explicitly lists exactly three (registry_integrity_notes, methodology.json timestamp_scope, registry_counts self-caveat). A defender asks: 'Which is the fourth?' Carry forward for batch resolution once ISS-1087 enumerates the full caveat stack.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; carry with c4.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c4 H6 unchanged. onclick="showTab('timestamp');return false" cancels the anchor jump so the reader lands at the top of the Timestamp Error tab rather than the April-2026 update block. Parallel issue in WIN-058 / WIN-058b. Cosmetic UX.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; refactor in a future UX pass across the WIN-058 / WIN-058b / WIN-062 triplet.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c4 H7 unchanged. detail_evidence still cites Tesla's '471,240 km/s (~1.57c)' alongside the dome's 1.574c headline, without noting that 471,240/299,792 = 1.5719c, so the dome's 1.574c is a re-expression using the fitted disc_diameter rather than Tesla's own ratio. Carry forward.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; batch with a one-sentence acknowledgement if evidence is edited.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN precision asymmetry (carry from c3). WIN-058 says 'three instances in one commit (6e06efb, 2026-04-17)', WIN-058b says 'three documentary concessions in a single 24-hour window', WIN-062 says 'one of at least three ... in a 24-hour window on 2026-04-17'. The claims are substantively consistent but the wording is not — a reader cross-checking the three WINs will notice. Not a new finding, but the c5 re-read surfaces that the WIN-062 patch did not synchronize phrasing with its siblings. Not blocking; flagging for a future consistency pass.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; batch with a cross-WIN language alignment when ISS-1087 closes.

stronger_arguments.description: Promote construction-by-fitting into the verdict text (carried unchanged from c4). The strongest anti-WIN-062 argument remains: the disc_diameter was fitted to globe circumference via test_curve_stretching.py / find_curve.py, so 1.574c is algebraically equivalent to (globe-circumference / Tesla-time) — globe physics relabeled, not an independent dome prediction. The current verdict paragraph 1 alludes to this but paragraph 2 goes entirely to the post-hoc angle. A defender who grants 'yes, the value is old' still has to explain why 'dome confirmation' equals 'known-globe-number divided by Tesla's measured time.' Promoting construction-by-fitting into paragraph 2 would make the verdict closing sentence bite harder than the carve-out citation does. Same recommendation as c4; not a fresh hole.
sources: data/wins.json WIN-062 detail_evidence (existing 'fitted to globe-derived WGS84 distances' language)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 58 (dome's own derivation uses r_disc, which was fitted)

stronger_arguments.description: Advocate-mode for the defender reading the patched paragraph. A smart defender will now say: 'Fine, you fixed your Tesla source attribution — thanks. But look at your own carve-out: you cite only condition (2). Even on your own rules, you have not shown your test fails on all three legs. And your chronology paragraph doesn't mention that a century-old measurement was private until 1978 — by your own timeline we have 48 years of public availability. Your post-hoc test is weaker than you're letting on.' The counter to both is available (condition (1) also fails; 'publicly accessible' vs 'measured' distinction is a word-game, not a substance-game) but the current text does not preempt these moves. The two minor tightening edits in holes 1 and 2 close both openings in roughly two sentences.

kernel_of_truth.description: The patched second paragraph is now factually correct on the Tesla chronology: the 1.574c velocity originates in Tesla's 1899 Colorado Springs experiments, the 1905 US Patent 787412 is the contemporaneous filing whose drawings depict a spherical Earth, and the value has been publicly available for decades before the dome catalog adopted it. The correct source attribution closes the biggest c4 rhetorical opening. The removal of 'verbatim' and the shift from 'patent value' to 'published velocity' eliminate two more openings. On structure, the c4 diagnosis that the post-hoc argument works cleanly for a monitoring=none WIN once the monitor-script boilerplate is stripped remains correct, and the current paragraph is the right shape.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The verdict (Misleading) rests primarily on paragraph 1's scientific critique (sphere drawings, waveguide physics, disc-diameter fitted to globe) and the detail_evidence construction-by-fitting argument. Paragraph 2's post-hoc test is additive. The remaining minor openings (carve-out conditions, sourcing-window wording, pre-registration phrase) do not touch the Misleading verdict — they are rhetorical tightening opportunities, not substantive challenges. Recommended action is minor_edit for optional cleanup, not major_rewrite.

deeper_analysis:
The c4 -> c5 transition is a clean example of self-apply working as designed. The decider's commit 20f6d44 rewrote the second paragraph of detail_verdict_text along exactly the lines c4 recommended (H1 hole 1 replacement text matches almost verbatim; H2 'verbatim' removed; H3 'patent value' -> 'published velocity'). The three c4 major/moderate holes are fully resolved. Verdict holds. Confidence rises from 0.85 (c4) to 0.9 (c5) because the main attribution error is gone and the remaining openings are all minor.

The c5 work is largely: (i) confirm the patch; (ii) re-read for new openings the patch may have introduced; (iii) inventory carry-forward minor items. Three potential new minor openings surface (carve-out conditions unsell, 'scientific record for over a century' loose wording, OTS/pre-registration phrasing). None are blocking; all are tightening opportunities.

Cross-WIN consistency notes: WIN-001 (Tesla 11.78 Hz, cited to US Patent 787412) and WIN-062 (Tesla 1.574c, cited to 1899 Colorado Springs Notes) now have correctly-disjoint Tesla-document attribution. The earlier verdict-text error where WIN-062 swapped in the patent as the velocity source is gone. WIN-058 / WIN-058b / WIN-062 have parallel EXP-181 acknowledge-without-fix paragraphs with slightly different precision language — not a new finding, carry forward.

Quantitative verification: Part 3.1b carve-out in data/sections.json still has three conjunctive conditions; the rewritten paragraph cites condition (2) verbatim. Tesla 471,240 km/s / 299,792 km/s = 1.5719c; dome 1.574c. Difference = 0.13% — consistent with the evidence's 0.24% rounding note between Tesla's 471,240 and the dome's 472,358. No new numerical issues introduced by the patch.

Holistic fit: the patched paragraph is now factually and rhetorically clean. A minor_edit pass addressing the three new minor items + c4 carry-forwards would tighten further, but the review is publishable as-is. No verdict_change or major_rewrite is warranted.

citations_verified: Tesla 1899 Colorado Springs Notes as source of 1.574c velocity — verified via raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207 (dome's own source attribution)
US Patent 787412 — confirmed 1905 grant on Google Patents; cited correctly in the patched text for the wave-propagation method and spherical-Earth drawings (NOT for the velocity value itself)
DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171 (Galejs 1966) — earlier-cycle verification of Earth-ionosphere waveguide phase velocity, unchanged
Part 3.1b anchor #posthoc-standard — still present in data/sections.json; verified reachable from the patched paragraph's href
Part 3.1b quoted fragment 'not plausibly known to the author at write-time' — verified verbatim in sections.json carve-out block (unchanged since c4)
Nikola Tesla Museum (Beograd) 1978 Colorado Springs Notes posthumous publication — consistent with the dome's own attribution and widely cited in Tesla literature; standard secondary source
citations_unchecked: Exact text of US Patent 787412 body for whether it contains any specific numerical velocity value (we rely on the dome's attribution of the velocity to the 1899 Notes, which our patched paragraph correctly reflects)
Independent verification of the 1978 Beograd publication as the first posthumous publication (widely-cited but not personally verified; not load-bearing for the Misleading verdict)

checked: WIN-001 vs WIN-062 Tesla-document attribution: after the patch, WIN-001 (11.78 Hz) correctly sources US Patent 787412 (1905) per raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54, and WIN-062 (1.574c) correctly sources 1899 Colorado Springs Notes per raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207. The c4 swap error is fixed.

WIN-058 / WIN-058b / WIN-062 EXP-181 acknowledge-without-fix paragraphs: substantively consistent (all three name the same 2026-04-17 commit and the same three concessions) but phrasing differs across the three WINs. No new finding; same carry-forward as c3 / c4.
inconsistencies_found: Cross-WIN phrasing: 'three instances in one commit (6e06efb, 2026-04-17)' (WIN-058) vs 'three documentary concessions in a single 24-hour window' (WIN-058b) vs 'one of at least three ... in a 24-hour window on 2026-04-17' (WIN-062). Same event, three phrasings. Non-blocking; batch for a language alignment pass.
